notes.note: A man whose death in 45 BC left legacies in which Caesar took a twelfth share and Cicero's friend Lepta a third. He is known only from this letter.

notes.anchor: Caesar comes in for an ounce
notes.note: Roman inheritances were reckoned in twelfths of the as (the whole estate); the uncia ("ounce") was one twelfth, the triens ("a third") four twelfths. So Caesar takes one twelfth of Babullius's estate, Lepta a full third.

notes.anchor: to hold on to the inheritance
notes.note: Under Caesar a legacy to a man of Lepta's standing could be at risk; entering on ("holding") a bequest required a formal act of acceptance, and Lepta fears his right to it may be challenged.
Porcia, daughter of Cato the Younger and wife of Marcus Brutus; the funeral eulogy (laudatio) is for her, and Cicero has revised his copy before it circulates among the family.
Cn. Domitius Ahenobarbus the Younger, son of the consul of 54 BC and a relation by marriage of the family mourning Porcia; later consul in 32 BC.

notes.anchor: the eulogies of Marcus Varro and Ollius
notes.note: Other funeral eulogies for Porcia, by the polymath M. Terentius Varro and by an otherwise unknown Ollius, which Cicero wants to read again. No entity exists for Ollius.
